title: Deque
description: Combining Sliding Window and Monotonic Stack into one: finding the maximum of every window of size k in O(n) using a monotonic deque.
objectives: Understand why sliding window maximum needs both ends of a deque
Trace the algorithm step by step
Understand why this achieves O(n) overall

sections.text: Sliding Window updated a running sum in O(n). Monotonic Stack kept a stack in sorted order for O(n) Next Greater Element. This problem needs both ideas working together, which is exactly why it needs deque instead of stack or queue alone — recall deque's own topic: fast operations at BOTH ends.
2. The Problem: Maximum of Every Window of Size k

The naive approach — recomputing the max of each window from scratch — is O(n × k). Sliding Window's subtract/add trick worked for sums, but doesn't directly work for max: when the maximum leaves the window, there's no cheap way to know the new maximum without looking again.

sections.title: For each new element
steps: Remove indices from the BACK whose values are smaller than the new element (they can never be the max again)
Add the new index to the back
Remove the FRONT index if it's fallen outside the current window
The front now holds the current window's maximum

sections.title: Why removing from the back is safe
points: If a new value is bigger than values already waiting at the back, those smaller values can NEVER become the maximum again — the new value is both bigger AND will stay in the window longer
This is exactly Monotonic Stack's core idea, applied to the back of a deque instead of a plain stack
The front holds the current maximum because the deque's values decrease front-to-back — it's monotonic

sections.text: Same amortized reasoning as Monotonic Stack and Queue: each index is pushed to the back at most once and popped (from either end) at most once across the entire run — O(n) total, not O(n × k).

sections.title: A leaderboard that discards anyone beaten by a newer, better score
sections.text: Imagine a leaderboard where, the instant a new high score is entered, every older score smaller than it is discarded immediately — they could never win again while this new one is around. The leaderboard's front always holds the current champion, and old scores are removed from the back the moment a bigger one shows up, while scores that have aged out of the relevant time window are removed from the front.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Trying to reuse Sliding Window's subtract/add sum trick directly for maximum — it doesn't transfer; max needs the monotonic deque approach instead.
Storing values instead of indices in the deque — indices are needed to detect when the front has aged out of the current window.
Forgetting to check window.front() <= i - k — without removing expired front indices, the maximum could come from outside the current window.
Only recording a result once the window is fully formed (i >= k - 1) — earlier iterations don't yet represent a complete window of size k.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: Why can't the sliding window sum technique be directly reused for sliding window maximum?
questions.answer: Because when the maximum element leaves the window, there's no O(1) way to know the new maximum without re-examining the remaining elements — unlike sum, which updates cleanly with a single subtract and add.

questions.question: Why does a monotonic deque work for sliding window maximum?
questions.answer: Removing smaller values from the back before adding a new one is safe because those smaller values can never become the maximum again while the larger new value remains in the window — keeping the deque's front always holding the current maximum.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the monotonic deque approach to sliding window maximum?
questions.answer: O(n) — each index is pushed and popped from the deque at most once across the entire algorithm, an amortized analysis matching Monotonic Stack's.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Sliding window maximum combines Sliding Window and Monotonic Stack: for each new element, pop smaller values from the back of a deque (they can never be the max again while the bigger new value is in the window), push the new index, then pop the front if it's aged out of the current k-sized window — the front always holds the current maximum. This needs deque specifically because it requires fast operations at both ends: popping from the back to maintain the monotonic order, and popping from the front to expire old indices. Despite the nested-looking logic, each index is pushed and popped at most once overall, giving O(n) total time.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Deque (Sliding Window Max)
sections.text: Monotonic deque = a leaderboard, new high score kicks out everyone smaller from the back. Front = current champion, expired front = aged out of the window.
